replies.text:
Me and a friend of mine did a fan edit of  Force Awakens back in 2015.  It took us 2 years to complete it, which we later titled it as Force Awakens Redux Cut.  We had no idea how episode 8 was going to turn out and yes the purpose of our cut was because we thought even back in 2015 that The Force Awakens was a terrible sequel.  So we did our very best to right a lot of wrongs.  

Here is a general list in no particular order of some the changes made in the Redux Cut of Star Wars Episode VII: The Force Awakens.


1	New Opening Crawl

2	Film is sequenced differently to feel less like Episode IV: A New Hope

3	Kylo Ren never removes his mask until his Encounter with Han Solo.

4	All dialogue or scenes from other characters revealing Kylo Ren’s real identity is removed or sequenced differently.

5	New Subtitles have been added.

6	New Additional Music has been added throughout especially scenes that never featured any music before.

7	Never before seen footage has been added.

8	Rey’s character and abilities have been altered quite a bit. “No Jedi Mind Trick” The cut does it’s best to humanize her and there are hints that she may possibly be a darksider.

9	New Footage of Darth Vader is added

10	Less is more with Finn. Finn’s character is not a comic relief.
11	The Film’s tone is much more serious.

12	Poe’s return is much more mysterious

13	Phasma raises the alarms when she is captured by Han and Finn

14	Han is not a Smuggler. NO RATHTARS. NO Kanji Club

15	Leia doesn’t hug Rey

16	The Final Battle is sequenced differently.

17	Most of the bad acting/dialogue has been removed

18	New Extended Ending.

19	Sadly Less is more with Han and Leia

20	There are many more suprises and easter eggs throughout the movie that differ from the original

Actually just rewatched and reckon it is still good, at that golden time when we were spoilt with the likes of Interstellar, Edge of Tomorrow etc. For some folks to say out of hand that the movie is 100% sht (because it diverged from cannon) without taking into consideration the acting, set pieces, settings, production, score, dialog (‘a droid stole the freighter?’), effects etc etc etc?… well, then they are not worth listening to and donkey '1/10' downvotes get overlooked by the aggregators. 
Is it so inconceivable that an entity somewhere in the universe (Rey, GD of Palpy) might have a special affinity with the Force? That first Falcon scene is epic, as was Han talking about the Force being true. There were many homages to cannon; it was all there.
The tards even got angry that there was a ginger or that Rey said the Millennium Falcon was garbage—which it was, it was JUST a suped up freighter, and which was what Luke & Leia originally alluded to.
Worst for me, and it is a small one, was the Scot… you cannot do accents beyond plain English, American, possibly a bit of French (for the aliens) as it brings folks back to our universe.

replies.author: @marcs.6913
replies.text:
There are still "fans", really?  Nobody who was there can forget how In 1977 we, along with half the known world, went ga-ga over the original films and by the time "Jedi" was made in 1983, the fans practically worshipped at George Lucas's feet, in sheer anticipation for the next brilliance that was certain to come out of Skywalker Ranch.  So I get no joy to admit I really, REALLY tried to like the prequel trilogy of 1999-2005 - but just couldn't.  So like for everybody else, it was sad for me to realize the magic was gone never to return, so even hoping for a repeat was fruitless.  
So after watching the 1999 film of the prequel trilogy, any fan with an IQ higher than room temperature had to face facts:  When not even George Lucas himself could make a decent Star Wars sequel, the chance that anybody else could do so was practically non-existent.

replies.author: @misterskippy2u
replies.text:
I gave RoS a shot, even after the disaster that was TLJ, which I watched once. I made it through about 15 minutes of RoS before turning it off and doing something better with my time. That was enough for me. I actually did watch The Force Awakens a few times, if only to better understand where and how the saga truly died. TFA is at least mildly entertaining.
What could have kept the saga alive:
1. Develop the character of Fin. So much potential story was there. Disney kicked it to the curb.
2. Give us a meaningful (not fan-service) on-screen reunion of the beloved heroes of the OT.
3. Keep the OT heroes true heroes, not has-beens who turned their backs on everything the Rebellion fought and died for. If the OT heroes die in the story to "pass the baton", they need to die fighting for what's right like heroes would.
4. Don't make Rey the GOAT at everything she does. She is not relatable to anyone.
5. Make Kylo Ren a truly terrifying figure. He may be strong in Dark Side Force abilities, but the way he's written makes him come across as a child having a tantrum. He is not a terrifying villain, he is an annoying villain. It can be said that Ep.I and Ep.II Anakin was an annoying whiner, but he was a literal child! By Ep.III, adult Anakin was someone you didn't want to mess with. In his first on-screen appearance, ANH Vader is terrifying and exudes power even though when he fought Obi-Wan he moved like an old man swatting bees with his cane.
In other words, rewrite the whole darn story for Ep. 7, 8, and 9.

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
@HyraxusPrimus 
*"That might be the biggest plot hole of the whole thing."*
Everything about Jake Skywalker being "missing" is part of the same massive plot hole.

1. (As the original comment asked.) Why did Jake leave a map in the first place?

2. If Jake didn't create the map, who did?

3. If R2 was napping the entire time Jake was gone, how did anyone even know the map existed in the first place?

4. Why was a small section cut out of the middle of the map?

5. One of the primary reasons X-Wings require astromech droids is to calculate hyperspace routes. How did Jake calculate his hyperspace route without an astromech droid?

6.  If Jake used a different astromech droid, why did R2 have the map at all?

7. In a galaxy with computers fast and powerful enough to calculate routes through hyperspace, requiring the ability to factor in the movement of a near infinite number of celestial objects to ensure the ship doesn't collide with anything's "mass shadow" in hyperspace, how the hell could no one look at the map fragment R2 had and figure out where it was in the galaxy?

The seventh one is the one that's bugged me the most since _The Farce Awokens_ came out. The map R2 has just has a section cut out. It shouldn't take much effort to align the section R2 has with a map of the galaxy and figure out which section is missing. It's not like the section R2 had was mostly empty, interstellar space; it shows HUNDREDS of stars (if not thousands). And the bit added by testicle droid has a much smaller number of stars to display. In _The Empire Strikes Back,_ we learn that the Empire has been sending _THOUSANDS_ of probe droids throughout the galaxy trying to find the Rebel's main base. Why didn't someone just send a probe droid to each of the stars / systems in the missing section of R2's map?

It's bad enough they basically erased him from the story, but to make the focus of the movie explicitly about finding him like he's some kind of superweapon...Jake Skywalker had 1 decent point, why would any of them think he'd just wave his hand and instantly win the war for them? Oh honey, bless your heart—you think Star Wars peaked before Disney because... what, you miss the era when George Lucas gave us Jar Jar Binks, wooden dialogue that could make a robot cringe, and a trilogy so reviled it drove fans to threaten Lucas' life? Cute nostalgia.

Fact check: The prequel trilogy grossed about $2.5 billion worldwide on lower budgets, sure, but Disney's sequels alone raked in over $4.4 billion, and the franchise as a whole has generated nearly $12 billion in profits since the acquisition (far outpacing the $4 billion Disney paid for Lucasfilm). That's billions more people actually showing up and paying to watch, not just complaining online.

And let's be real: the prequels were hated on release—critics and fans alike called them soulless cash-grabs with zero chemistry. Disney revived a dying franchise, brought in diverse heroes (because apparently the Force isn't just for straight white dudes anymore), and gave us gems like Rogue One, Andor, and The Mandalorian that actually respect the lore without midichlorian pseudoscience nonsense.

If Disney hadn't stepped in, we'd still be stuck with no new content, rehashing the originals forever. But go off about "ruining" it while ignoring how corporate greed funded your childhood in the first place. The empire strikes back... with actual box office success.

replies.author: @rizzardbro
replies.text:
Even if some of George Lucas’ lore should be fairly criticized, and Disney brought a new level of popularity to Star Wars that we’ve never seen before, it doesn’t take away from the fact that Star Wars is at its most stale and divisive point. 

You are right that Disney’s Star Wars  had racked in waves of money and fans that George Lucas’ prequel trilogy was unable come close with. However, to say Disney’s handling of Star Wars “peaked” over George Lucas’ based on 1) money; 2) sequel trilogy; and 3) (lackluster) content is quite absurd.

First, you said that Disney’s sequel trilogy brought in $4.4 billion and generated $12 billion in profits to generously make up for the $4 billion price tag for Lucasfilm. While Disney’s Star Wars sequel trilogy grossed roughly $4.4 billion at the global box office, gross revenue isn’t the same as actual profit, and the generated $12 billion in profits is incorrect because the figure “aggregate[s] 10-year revenue streams both generated and expected” until 2027. What it means for the $4.4 billion global box office revenue and the generated $12 billion in profits you claim is false. The $4.4 billion box office revenue made from the sequel trilogy films produced (with the addition of Solo and Rogue One) only $1.0-1.2 billion in net theatrical profit, which is $2.8 billion short of recouping the $4 billion Lucasfilm acquisition. The $12 billion in profits is an estimated value that doesn’t exist. Since it excludes certain losses and expenses that Disney simply doesn’t want to mention, they offered a total amount of profits that fundamentally isn’t real and not supported by supported financial breakdowns.

Second, you asked the other commenter if they missed the era of the “soulless cash-grab” trilogy, bad dialogue, and Jar Jar Binks. But is Disney’s soulless, cash-grab trilogy really better? I will pick the former. You can’t use the box office revenue argument anymore because it doesn’t consider the actual profit and its profitability for Disney. Instead, you can defend the trilogy that was unfinished from start to finish (don’t bring up Lucas’ unplanned trilogies because it was significantly better than Disney’s), switched from director to director (Abrams —> Johnson —> Abrams), riddled with bad characters (General Hux, Han Solo, Rose Tico, Rey, Amilyn Hodo, Luke Skywalker, etc.), lazy fight choreography (Throne room scene in TLJ; entirety of TROS), terrible dialogue (“Somehow Palpatine returned;” “I’m the spy;” “They fly now? They fly now!;” “I am all the Sith! … And, I’m all the Jedi!), pushed aside its diverse characters (Finn - FN-2187; Rose Tico; Lando Calrissian; DJ) for more profit, and left a gaping hole for Disney to lose money in if anymore of their Star Wars projects fail. While I will discuss the lackluster content Disney has been producing recently, it’s really important to address that liking the sequel trilogy isn’t a bad thing; it’s bad, however, when it is praised to be better than George Lucas’ works despite the fact that the sequel trilogy destroyed an IP that could have made trillions in profit. Disney’s revival of the franchise met its end pretty quick when the same things you condemn Lucas’ prequels for has the same, if not, more mistakes you’ve mentioned. I would take Jar Jar Binks everyday of the week if that meant Disney wouldn’t have drop the ball on reviving this beloved franchise.

Third and last, the content you mentioned, while good, is seriously outweighed by all of the bad/unprofitable content Disney has produced over the years. To give you a list: Kenobi, Solo: A Star Wars Story, the Acolyte, the Book of Boba Fett, Tales of the Empire, Tales of the Underworld, Mandalorian Season 3. If Rogue One, Andor, and Mandalorian season 1 & 2, and maybe Ahsoka, are the crutches Disney has to lean on to keep Star Wars afloat, then there’s a serious issue with the way they run this property. Like how does Kenobi, Solo, and Boba Fett not elevate Disney’s Star Wars?! It’s because the sequel trilogy has set a new low for Star Wars like how Disney set a new high for Star Wars through their global accumulation of fans and money. “It’s like poetry; it rhymes.” Even when you consider Star Wars video games, toys, comics, etc., those avenues keep funneling money into an IP that keeps losing profit, and if that pit isn’t closed through some miraculous change to Star Wars, then I’m afraid the profits will continue to decrease.

In consideration of the money, the sequel trilogy, and the content Disney has produced, gained, and lost, Disney might have saved a “dying franchise,” but forever stained a beloved franchise by making it nearly impossible to gain profit from with its panned sequel trilogy and content that followed. You may continue to defend this mega empire of a company and its sequel trilogy, but you cannot deny the evidence that this IP’s profits from movies and tv shows have been on a steady decline ever since. The empire strikes back… and loses once again. Empire: 0; Rebels: 2.

author: @MrOffTrail
text:
The most important aspect of Mystery Box writing is that the plot is hidden not only from the audience, but from the writers and directors or showrunners as well.  The goal at first is just to get people hooked enough to invest in the story, and then build the rest of airplane while flying it.  They put up the metaphorical box, and around it the place a bunch of story hooks:  mysterious inexplicable things that a normal writer would have used to enage the audience in their story.  But in a Mystery Box, there is no story, just the Box.  When Jar Jar created “Lost”, he had no clue what the story would be.  All he had was a set of hooks: plane crashes mysteriously on a desert island.  Mysterious hatch.  Mysterious numbers.  Mysterious black thing.  Polar bears mysteriously on a tropical island. Crippled man mysteriously can walk.   And that’s it.  If the show gets picked up, we’ll worry about what it all means later.  If we can’t make all these things make sense, “Oh well.  Nobody will care”.  “What’s that box?”  Oh, erm, don’t look at that, here, look at these jangling keys.”  And if they do, we already got paid.  If it doesn’t get picked up, we didn’t waste any time writing a story.   

The problem is, they can never pull it off.  They can never tie up the loose ends, and can never create a meaningful story after the fact.  The individual episodes are internally contradicting,  and the later episodes contadict the previous ones, and abandon plot threads they can’t resolve.  

The Disney sequels being a Mystery Box is Jar Jar’s fault, and all that comes with that.  Why haven’t we held him accountable for this fraudulent writing style, and all the other writers who adopted it thoughout modern Hollywood? The studios don’t care because Lost and The Force Awakens made a lot of money, because after decades of proper writing, nobody thought anyone would have the audacity to commit fraud on such a massive scale, much less twice.  But we should know better by now, when this has become the norm.  “Fallout” is a particularly egregious recent example.  I’ll be shocked if “Severence” isn’t a Mystery Box too, but I still have hope for that one.  My heart may be broken though.  We will know in the end, because it always becomes obvious in the end.

But Mystery Box writing only part of the problem with the Disney Sequels.  Everything else is Kathleen Kennedy’s fault:  the Mary Sueism, the feminist iconoclasm, the inconsistent casting, the overall lack of vision, just to name a few of the problems.

@ChaptermasterPedroKantor-kv5yw 
*"...the concept of the Prequels that people hated the most, midichlorians..."*
Yeah...that's not the most hated thing about the prequels. It's certainly one of the things many people hated, but the wooden dialogue and asinine "romance" between Anakin and Padme were more hated.

That being said, I don't think Lucas' Episodes VII - IX would have been great. He started losing his touch with some of the decisions he made for _Return of the Jedi._

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
@ardendragoon 
*"and it still would have been 1000x better than the duhsney slop."*
Which doesn't actually mean anything. Even if it was better than Disney's Suckwell Travesty, it still could have been complete sh*t. Saying Lucas' trilogy would have been better is like saying getting kicked in the balls once is "better" than being kicked in the balls twice.

*"It made perfect sense that the jedi had a way to test prospective students."*
But it _DIDN'T_ make sense within the universe. All the way back in 1977 we saw _ON SCREEN_ that Force-users could sense Force potential in others by simply being close to them. There was absolutely no need to introduce midichlorians as a way to explain how Jedi identified prospective students. (And that's absolutely *NOT* why Lucas introduced the concept.) Furthermore, as far back as 1994 (and possibly sooner; this is the earlies I can recall), in _Jedi Search,_ by Kevin J. Anderson, there was a special scanner that non Force-users could use to determine if someone had Force potential. It was never explicitly stated that the scanner was searching for midichlorians, and no one needed that level of detail...because we accepted it as "space magic," and moved on.

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
@HULK-HOGAN1 
*" the Force is spiritual essence. Not little creatures living in people."*
As pointed out by someone else, midichlorians aren't the Force. They're simply the mechanism by which other lifeforms can access the Force.

*"I love the Prequels but that idea was dumb."*
It _was_ (and still is) dumb...but at least Lucas had a reason for it. It never comes out explicitly in the films, but he's said on multiple occasions since the release of the prequels that he needed a way to explain why Vader didn't simply turn on the Emperor and take over, because Anakin is supposed to be one of (if not the absolute) most powerful Force-users of all time. If he's so much more powerful than Palpatine, why wouldn't he just take over? In Empire, it's clear Vader wants to overthrow the emperor, so why not do it? Before the prequels, we all just assumed it was because the Emperor was an incredibly powerful Force-user (which he was), and Vader just didn't measure up. replies.text:
@AbstractM0use 
*" It's in the blood. It's in the blood."*
Midichlorians are in *ALL* cells, not just blood.

*"Yet it runs strong in families, suggesting a biological tie."*
First, the only evidence to support this claim is the fact that Anakin had two children who also had strong Force potential. Since the rest of the prequels make it explicitly clear that Jedi aren't to be involved in any kind of relationship that forms attachment, it seems pretty obvious that having children would be off limits (in general; exceptions exist - see Ki-Adi-Mundi.) 

Second, it suggests a nonsensical biological connection, extremely poorly executed. Hell, it was barely even thought out before it was introduced, and created more plot holes than it patched.
Midichlorians are described as microscopic lifeforms that inhabit other lifeforms' cells. As a separate lifeform, the midichlorians would be dependent upon their own biological processes to replicate themselves. Or they'd have to hijack the host's genetics to replicate themselves (like a virus). If Anakin's midichlorian count is "off the chart" at more than 20,000, and if the midichlorians are passed along from parent to offspring, shouldn't Luke and Leia have the same count? We're not talking about genes that are established by blending both parents' genetics; we're talking about a separate lifeform that resides within their cells, meaning the two sperm cells that fertilized the eggs that became Luke and Leia both had midichlorian counts of more than 20,000. Furthermore, while we can explain that Anakin has that high a count because he was (allegedly) conceived by the midichlorians themselves, how does this biological explanation account for all of the Jedi children who don't have Jedi parents? How do two beings with insufficient midichlorians to grant them Force-sensitivity combine to make an offspring who can touch the Force? Again, it's not genes. Two people who have the recessive gene for red hair can have a child with red hair if the genes align properly during conception, even if no one else in their extended family has had red hair for 200 years. But there can't be a gene that dictates the number of a foreign lifeform will reside in your cells. 


And, of course, it's heavily implied in the films (and explicitly stated in the EU) that the number of Jedi in the galaxy has been dwindling for at least a few hundred years. If the ability to use the Force is so easily passed from one generation to the next, why aren't the Jedi cloning themselves? Or, barring cloning, why isn't it a requirement for all Jedi to provide reproductive cells to the Order for IVF, then pay surrogates to carry the offspring to term, thereby ensuring there are always enough Jedi to go around? 

The biological "explanation" is so poorly thought out, it's laughable in it's stup!dity.

While simultaneously creating very real plot holes that make everything the Jedi believe and do completely nonsensical, if not criminally stup!d. Or do you not remember the Clone Wars? The Star Wars galaxy has advanced cloning technology to the point they can not only create perfect replicas, but they can alter clones' genetics enough to accelerate aging, fundamentally change the template's personality to modify behavior, and implant hard-wired instructions into a clone's mind. They could simply clone Jedi, and since a Jedi's connection to the Force is purely biological, their clones would have the same connection to the Force. replies.author: @AnonymousNoNayme
replies.text:
@JackFischer115 Ftr i enjoy the joke myself...but i gotta set the record straight once and for all because some people actually believe it. Die hard is not a Christmas movie. It's an action movie that just so happens to take place during Christmas.

Don't worry, i'll explain:

This is a fact because there are Christmas movies that are also action movies that put the Christmas theme first (Jingle All The Way, Violent Night, Red One, Fatman, Home Alone, etc.)

Case in point, nobody calls Goodfellas a Christmas movie. It's a gangster movie that takes place during Christmas (just like how Die Hard is an action movie that takes place during Christmas).

There is no valid counter-argument to this assessment other than "it's subjective bro" (which doesn't logically refute the argument i just made which proves otherwise). In logical debate that type of appeal to subjectivity is known as the "subjectivist fallacy". Pretentious modern art d-bags love that one😅(guess what i think about people that say art and music quality is subjective😏).
